edges.explanation: 26,000-38,000 equivalent deaths with progress slowed proves the UK is failing on air quality targets
The scale of air pollution deaths directly justifies a Clean Air Act with WHO-standard legally binding limits
Deaths continuing despite targets proves targets without enforcement are meaningless
OEP finding government 'largely off track' with 62% seabird decline proves failure across biodiversity metrics
Being off track on biodiversity targets directly justifies the 30 by 30 enforcement policy
Off track on every metric is the primary evidence for the targets-without-delivery claim
71% of land being farmed with agriculture driving biodiversity loss and water pollution proves farming transition is the most important lever
Agriculture being the dominant land use and pollution source justifies doubling the ELM budget
Dividends extracted while infrastructure degrades and enforcement cut proves regulation alone without structural reform cannot work
Water company failure with dividends over infrastructure directly justifies structural reform with dividend ban
Dasgupta Review and £700m pollination value prove nature's economic value is enormous and systematically underpriced
Quantified ecosystem service values prove nature is infrastructure priced at zero
£20bn/yr in air pollution costs directly sizes the Clean Air Act's economic justification
£20bn/yr health costs from pollution that targets fail to address proves nature is infrastructure with quantifiable economic impact
Nature-based solutions being 50-75% cheaper than engineered alternatives proves environmental protection is economically rational
Cheaper nature-based flood defence proves nature provides infrastructure services underpriced by current accounting
Biodiversity decline posing 2008-level systemic risk proves nature loss has quantifiable economic consequences
Systemic financial risk from nature loss proves the infrastructure metaphor is not rhetorical but literal
Off track on every target directly supports the targets-without-delivery claim
Regulation alone being insufficient supports structural water company reform
Farming being both productive and nature-friendly with sufficient investment supports doubling the ELM budget
Nutrient neutrality being necessary because waterways are polluted supports maintaining and enforcing biodiversity protections
Quantified economic value of nature directly supports the nature-as-infrastructure claim
Doubling the ELM budget depends on the claim that nature provides infrastructure services worth funding
30 by 30 protection depends on the claim that nature is infrastructure worth preserving
Water company reform depends on the claim that targets without enforcement are meaningless
Clean Air Act depends on the claim that current air quality targets have failed to deliver
£1bn extra focused on food production risks undermining the nature-friendly transition by returning to direct subsidies
Abolishing nutrient neutrality removes protection the evidence says is needed until pollution sources are fixed
Making ELM work for farmers and nature aligns with the evidence but without commitment to budget doubling
Meeting Environment Act targets and maintaining BNG aligns with 30 by 30 though delivery is off track
Water company reform and increased regulator funding directly match the evidence-based policy
Tree planting and fuel poverty measures contribute to air quality and environmental restoration
Tripling farming budget and 50% pesticide reduction exceeds the evidence-based minimum and matches the direction
Rights of Nature and ending factory farming go beyond but align with the biodiversity protection evidence
Stopping Natural England enforcement and dropping EU environmental laws would remove the protections needed to address water and nature crises
Scrapping nature-friendly farming subsidies directly contradicts the evidence that ELM budget should be doubled
Dropping environmental laws would violate UK international commitments and trigger biodiversity decline with 2008-level economic risk
Already off track on every target while Reform proposes removing protections would accelerate ecological collapse
Water company reform reducing agricultural runoff pollution enables the farming transition by addressing downstream impacts
Sewage discharges increasing and waterways already stressed proves nutrient neutrality rules exist for a reason and cannot be safely abolished

cross_domain_edges.explanation: 26,000-38,000 equivalent deaths from air pollution is a major contributor to NHS burden comparable to the waiting list crisis
£20bn/yr air pollution health costs are part of the NHS funding gap that tax reform aims to close
Doubling ELM budget at £3bn/yr additional draws from the same constrained fiscal space as every other domain
Nature-based flood defence being cheaper supports housing development when combined with environmental protection rather than opposing it
Nutrient neutrality being necessary proves that planning reform must address pollution not just remove protections to unlock development
Peatland carbon sequestration at 3.2bn tonnes supports the energy domain's finding that insulation and nature-based demand reduction are the cheapest interventions
